note_data.title: Introduction to accounting
keyPoints: **Accounting** is the recording, classifying and summarizing of financial transactions and communicating the results to interested parties
The main purpose of accounting is to **provide financial information** to help users make informed economic decisions
**Internal users** include managers, owners and employees who need information for planning, controlling and decision-making
**External users** include investors, lenders, suppliers, customers and government who need information to assess financial performance
Accounting provides information about **financial position** (what the business owns and owes) and **financial performance** (profit or loss)
**Stewardship** means managers are accountable to owners for how they have used the business resources
Accounting helps in **planning** future activities, **controlling** current operations and **decision-making** for the business
Financial information must be **relevant, reliable, comparable and understandable** to be useful to users

definitions.definition: The recording, classifying and summarizing of financial transactions and communicating the results to interested parties
People within the business who use accounting information for planning, controlling and decision-making
People outside the business who use accounting information to make economic decisions about the business
The responsibility of managers to account to owners for how they have used the business resources
What the business owns (assets) and what it owes (liabilities) at a particular point in time

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Name or select the correct answer from information given
Give the main features or characteristics without explanation

modelAnswer.question: Explain two purposes of accounting. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: One purpose is to provide financial information to users because this helps them make informed economic decisions about the business. Another purpose is stewardship because managers need to account to owners for how they have used the business resources.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Provide financial information to users
Mark 2: To help make informed economic decisions
Mark 4: Managers account to owners for use of resources

commonMistakes: Never say accounting is 'just bookkeeping' — always say it includes communicating results to users
Students often confuse internal and external users — remember managers are internal, investors are external
Don't say accounting 'makes profit' — say it 'measures and reports profit'
Never give examples as definitions — define the term then give examples separately
Students forget that stewardship means accountability to owners, not just managing resources
examinerTips: This topic appears every year as foundation knowledge for other topics
Always distinguish between recording transactions and communicating results
Questions often ask for users and their information needs — learn specific examples
Purpose questions need the reason WHY accounting is done, not just WHAT it does
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Accounting process flow: Financial transactions → Recording → Classifying → Summarizing → Communicating to internal users (managers, owners, employees) and external users (investors, lenders, suppliers, government)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — they asked students to identify which accounting concept states that revenue can only be recognised after it has been earned
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q4b (6 marks) — candidates had to explain why businesses should make provision for depreciation and state the relevant accounting concept
This topic was examined in M/J 2023 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — students needed to identify which accounting concepts apply when goods are sold on 31 March but invoiced 3 days later
This concept was tested in O/N 2022 P2 Q5c (4 marks) — candidates were required to explain prior period adjustments with reference to relevant IAS and accounting treatment

workedExample.question: Explain why a business should make provision for depreciation, stating the relevant accounting concept. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define depreciation as the allocation of an asset's cost over its useful life
Step 2: Identify the matching concept (also known as accruals concept) as the relevant principle
Step 3: Explain that the matching concept requires expenses to be matched with the revenue they help generate in the same accounting period
Step 4: Apply this to depreciation - the asset generates revenue over multiple years, so its cost should be spread over those years
Step 5: State that this ensures fair presentation of profit and prevents overstating profit in the year of purchase
workedExample.answer: Businesses should make provision for depreciation to comply with the matching concept. This concept requires that expenses are matched with the revenues they help to generate in the same accounting period. Since non-current assets like machinery and equipment are used to generate revenue over several years, their cost should be allocated over their useful life rather than charged entirely in the year of purchase. This ensures that each year bears a fair share of the asset's cost, preventing the overstatement of profit in the purchase year and providing a more accurate measure of annual performance.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for identifying matching/accruals concept, 2 marks for explaining the concept, 2 marks for applying it to depreciation, 1 mark for stating the benefit of fair profit measurement

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the prudence concept with the matching concept when explaining depreciation' — always clearly state which specific accounting concept applies and define it accurately

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often failed to explain HOW the concept applies to the specific scenario' — don't just define concepts, always explain their practical application to the business situation given

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently mixed up revenue recognition and expense recognition principles' — remember that revenue recognition (realization concept) deals with when to record income, while matching deals with when to record expenses
note_data.keyDiagram: Students should be able to draw the Accounting Concepts Framework diagram showing the four fundamental concepts in interconnected boxes: Going Concern (top), Accruals/Matching (left), Prudence/Conservatism (right), and Consistency (bottom). Arrows should connect all concepts showing they work together. Each box should include a brief definition and example. This diagram often appears in 4-6 mark questions asking students to 'illustrate' or 'demonstrate understanding' of accounting concepts.
note_data.quickRecap: Accounting concepts are fundamental principles that guide financial reporting. The four key concepts are: Going Concern (business continues operating), Accruals/Matching (match revenues with related expenses in same period), Prudence (don't overstate assets/income, do provide for losses), and Consistency (use same methods year after year). These concepts ensure financial statements are reliable, comparable and give a true and fair view of business performance.
